# CST8916 Midterm  
## Use Case 3: Real-time stock trading application
### Team:  
**Divyang Lodariya**  
**Hesheng Yang**  
**Todd O’Neil**

### Section 1 - REST and GraphQL for Data Requests and Updates:
<p>
In the stock app, users need to fetch and update data like stock details, user portfolios, or settings. Both REST and GraphQL can manage these requests and updates, but they work in different ways. Here I will discuss methods in detail.   
</p>
<p>   
REST uses fixed endpoints and standard HTTP methods like GET (read), POST (create), PUT (update), and DELETE (remove). Every request done by a user goes to a specific address and uses simple commands like GET (read) or POST (to add) and the user will get that information. For example if a user wants to get stock information, then he will make a request like “GET /api/stocks/AAPL” in result he will get price, volume, his holdings and other information about Apple stock. So, in our app, the mobile or web client can make these types of calls when the user opens the app or clicks a button. The best benefit of REST is that it is very simple and easy to understand. But we also have some cons too and one of the cons is over-fetching. If a user only wants the price, REST might still send extra info like company description. For complex views like price + history + news, the app might need 3-4 separate requests, which slows things down and uses more data which is not ideal for users on phones.    
</p>
<p>   
On the other hand, GraphQL uses one single endpoint and lets the client ask exactly for the data it needs in a flexible "query" language. It is like telling a chef: "Give me only these items on my plate”, for example if a user makes a request in a query form “query { stock (symbol: "AC") { currentPrice }}” then user will get only current price of the stock nothing else. Therefore, GraphQL is great for our app because users have different needs: one person wants just prices, another wants prices + portfolio value + alerts and users will get only information what they ask for. This avoids wasting data and reduces the number of requests, making the app faster and better on mobile networks. The setup of GraphQL is a little bit harder than REST, even customers have to learn query language to use our stock application, but it uses very less internet and that makes the application faster.    
</p>
<p>   
In short, Both technologies can fully handle all data requests and updates in our stock market app. REST is simpler and easier to start with, while GraphQL is more flexible and efficient when users need different combinations of data. 
</p>

### Section 2 - WebSockets for Real-Time Communication:
<p>
WebSockets are the perfect choice for our real‑time stock market monitoring system. They allow the server to push updates to clients instantly over a persistent, full-duplex (bidirectional) connection. Unlike HTTP polling, where the client repeatedly asks the server for new data in static intervals, WebSockets keep a single open channel so updates can be delivered the moment they occur. This type of connection can be a key factor in the success or failure of any particular stock trade. Prices often change multiple times per second and if the client were relying on HTTP polling where say polling frequency was set to every 15 seconds, the user looking for a certain price to execute a trade might miss the window they were looking for, which is especially devastating for sell orders when trying to take profits.
</p>
<p>
With a WebSocket connection, the backend would maintain live connections to all connected users. When the user clients make their initial connection request they send connection upgrade headers attached telling the server they want to initiate a persistent connection, if the server supports WebSockets the request is granted and the connection is upgraded. Whenever the stock data provider publishes a new price, the server broadcasts that update to every subscribed client through WebSockets. For example, if a user is watching the AC ticker symbol (Air Canada), the server can immediately push a json message containing the symbol name, new price, and how much it changed. 
</p>
<p>
Dataframes sent via Websocket are headerless, which makes them very efficient for data transfer. This ensures the UI updates instantly without the user refreshing or waiting for the next polling interval, as data can be updated multiple times per minute.
</p>
<p>
WebSockets are also useful for tracking stock portfolios and sending alerts with the ability to track changes of multiple symbols at once.  For example if a user had a portfolio made up of 6 different symbols, they could be alerted if their overall portfolio balance dropped a certain percentage as the prices are recalculated as new data flows into the server. Overall, WebSockets provide low‑latency, event‑driven communication that allows a stock monitoring app to be fast, responsive and competitive.
</p>

### Section 3 - Technology Recommendation and Justification:
<p>
In the project of Real-time stock trading application, we are designing a real-time stock trading application, the system must provide low latency, high availability, secure transactions, and horizontal scalability. 
</p>
<p>
As design intent,  I would like recommend a hybrid architecture using GraphQL for flexible data queries, REST for transactional operations, and WebSockets for real-time communication, deployed on Microsoft Azure.
</p>
<p>
GraphQL should serve as the primary query layer. In a trading app, users request different combinations of data such as current price, portfolio value, and order history. GraphQL allows clients to retrieve exactly the required fields in a single request, reducing over-fetching and minimizing network latency. The GraphQL API can be deployed using Azure App Service and managed securely through Azure API Management for authentication, rate limiting, and monitoring.
</p>
<p>
I would like suggest use REST APIs for critical transactional operations such as buy/sell orders, authentication, and account updates. These operations require strict validation and ACID compliance. REST services can run in Azure App Service or Azure Kubernetes Service, while trade records are stored in Azure SQL Database to ensure consistency, automated backups, and high availability.
</p>
<p>
For live stock price updates, I would like suggest use WebSockets to handle the critical operation requirement.  HTTP polling introduces delay and unnecessary server load. Using Azure Web PubSub enables persistent bidirectional communication, allowing instant broadcasting of price changes and portfolio updates to thousands of connected users.
</p>
<p>
To improve reliability and decouple services, an event-driven design using Azure Service Bus is recommended. Trade requests can be queued, processed asynchronously, and confirmed through real-time notifications.
</p>
<p>
Overall, this is a hybrid architect based on Azure, whch ensures scalability, low latency, secure financial transactions, and efficient data delivery. It aligns with cloud-native design principles and supports peak trading loads while maintaining performance and reliability.
</p>

### Section 4 - Architecture Diagram: 
![Architecture](/img/cst8916-midterm-architecture.png)

### Here is a brief explanation for the project architecture diagram: 
<p>    
The architecture represents the cloud-based, event-driven design for a real-time stock trading application deployed on Microsoft Azure.   
</p>
<p>  
The Client (web or mobile app) connects to the backend through secure HTTPS and WebSocket connections. All traffic first passes through the API Gateway / Load Balancer, which handles request routing, authentication, rate limiting, and traffic distribution. In Azure, this can be implemented using Azure API Management with Azure Front Door or Application Gateway.   
</p>
<p>  
The gateway routes requests to two core services. The REST API manages transactional operations such as login, buy/sell orders, and account updates. These services can run on Azure App Service or Azure Kubernetes Service and store financial records in Azure SQL Database to ensure ACID compliance and high availability.   
</p>
<p>  
The WebSocket Server handles real-time communication. Using Azure Web PubSub, it maintains persistent connections and instantly pushes stock price updates and portfolio changes to connected users.   
</p>
<p>  
A Message Broker (Azure Event Hubs or Service Bus) enables asynchronous communication. Market data from the external Market Data Provider API is published to the broker, processed by backend services, and broadcast to clients via WebSockets.   
</p>
<p>  
This architecture ensures scalability, low latency, reliability, and secure trade processing. 
</p>
